Curriculum → Data storage and indexing → Replication and failover

Lesson 2 of 5

Replication and failover

Replicas improve read scale, durability, and recovery options, but they introduce lag, failover complexity, and write ownership decisions.

25-35 min Data storage and indexing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

A lot of interview depth comes from acknowledging what replicas do during steady state, incident response, and recovery.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Master-replica patterns Asynchronous replication improves availability but can lose the latest writes on failover2. Failover choices Use health checks and quorum-aware promotion when possible3. Recovery and repair Throttle catch-up traffic if necessary

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing A lot of interview depth comes from acknowledging what replicas do during steady state, incident response, and recovery. 2. Master-replica patterns Asynchronous replication improves availability but can lose the latest writes on failover3. Failover choices Use health checks and quorum-aware promotion when possible4. Recovery and repair Throttle catch-up traffic if necessary
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

A lot of interview depth comes from acknowledging what replicas do during steady state, incident response, and recovery.

What to say first

  • Explain the write path and which node is authoritative.
  • Discuss replica lag and user-visible effects.
  • Define failover triggers, RPO, and RTO.
2

Study section

Master-replica patterns

Single-writer topologies simplify conflict handling, while multi-writer topologies trade simplicity for write locality or availability.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Asynchronous replication improves availability but can lose the latest writes on failover
  • Synchronous replication improves durability but increases latency
  • Read replicas are great until replica lag becomes user-visible
3

Study section

Failover choices

Automatic failover shortens outages but must guard against split brain and stale leaders. Manual failover is slower but sometimes safer.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use health checks and quorum-aware promotion when possible
  • Clearly define RPO and RTO expectations
  • Plan how clients discover the new primary
4

Study section

Recovery and repair

After failover, the hard part is often reconciliation, replica catch-up, and preventing overloaded survivors from collapsing.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Throttle catch-up traffic if necessary
  • Protect hot partitions during recovery
  • Observe replication lag and failover drills regularly

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

Replication improves read scale and recovery options but introduces lag and failover choices.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Explain the write path and which node is authoritative.
  • Discuss replica lag and user-visible effects.
  • Define failover triggers, RPO, and RTO.
  • Mention recovery traffic and split-brain protection.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Saying replicas automatically improve everything.
  • Ignoring promotion safety and stale reads after failover.
  • Forgetting how clients find the new primary.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • When is asynchronous replication acceptable?
  • How would you explain replica lag to a product manager?
  • What changes if you need active-active writes across regions?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs Foundations and estimationMulti-region design and disaster recovery Security, multi-region, and operations

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Replication and failover

Use the simulator to see when primary, replica, shard, or queue pressure should change your storage story. Apply it to replication and failover so the trade-offs feel concrete.

8 nodes · 7 links

● Line 1: edge · 100000 rps · 4 ms● Line 2: service · 38000 rps · 10 ms● Line 3: cache · 120000 rps · 3 ms

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept

0 headings · 0 bullets · 0 words

⚠ Line 1: Start by adding at least one heading so the answer has a visible structure.

Explain the concept

Teach "Replication and failover" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • When is asynchronous replication acceptable?
  • Master-replica patterns
  • Failover choices
  • Explain the write path and which node is authoritative.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
